original_abstract:
This article explores the theological relationship between the ministry of church revitalisation and the sovereign work of the Holy Spirit. While contemporary ecclesiology often emphasizes pragmatic strategies and human methodologies, the author argues that the Holy Spirit is the primary agent of renewal, with church leaders serving as secondary agents who must cooperate with His divine initiative. By examining New Testament precedents—specifically the mandates for empowerment found in the book of Acts—the study establishes a framework for "spiritual competence" that rests not on natural talent, but on a living relationship with the Triune God. 

The author further outlines a methodology for revitalisation that aligns with the Spirit’s habitual modes of operation: the bold communication of the Word of Christ and the conviction of sin. Moving beyond sensationalism or purely numerical metrics, the article identifies several qualitative markers of biblical empowerment, including authentic holiness in leadership, communal repentance, and a renewed, Christ-centered focus within the local congregation. Ultimately, the paper suggests that lasting church renewal occurs when leaders move away from "golden key" pragmatism toward a humble, expectant dependence on the Spirit’s work through established biblical means.
humanized_abstract:
This article explores the relationship between church revitaluation ministry and the sovereign Holy Spirit. Where much of contemporary ecclesiology seems to dwell on the mechanics and methodologies of church renewal, the minister is to be aware of the paramount role of the Holy Spirit in any real revival or renewal. Drawing on scriptural precedents from the book of Acts, which is replete with evangelistic and disciplinary imperatives to empower the church for its unique commission, this paper attempts to provide some parameters for the concept of “spiritual competence” that do not depend upon the ability of men, but upon men’s awareness of their on-going dependence upon the triune God.

In the third section, the author suggests an approach to renewal consistent with the methodology of the Spirit using the Word of Christ for conviction of sin. This approach is distinguishable from what the author calls sensationalism and an exclusive focus on numbers of converts and decisions. Features of biblical revitalization highlighted by the author include (1) personal holiness on the part of Christian leaders, (2) corporate repentance, and (3) a new focus on Christ in the local church. The thrust of the paper is to call church leaders away from what the author terms a “golden key” type of pragmatic approach to revival to trusting the Spirit for renewal through means consistent with Scripture.
